speaker: James
text: Yeah. So a lot of times these landlords will have a form lease that they'll send to each tenant, and the stuff that deviates from that form... Not always, but a lot of times will either be all caps bolded, italicized, or the format... It just looks different. You can tell that it was added on. So anything like that is usually what I keep an eye out for. It's helpful once you've read a couple of these leases by the landlord to see what their form lease looks like so that you can spot deviations. But if anything like that, I usually try to jot down... This lease was signed at a high rent, but it was because the landlord gave a really big TI allowance, which is just the TI to build out their space divided by their square footage would be the TI foot. So that's something I like to flag. I like to flag if there's ever been any rent that was waived where maybe during COVID, they were delayed on rent. So they worked on a payment plan. I'd like to flag that. I like to flag if when they renewed, say they were paying $10 a foot, and then they renewed most recently, but it was a decrease, so it was down to $9 a foot. That I usually like to flag. I try to flag any big jumps where, if they were paying $5, now they're paying $15 because you don't know how well they'll be able to sustain that. So basically anything like that where my goal on those notes is just to flag any deviations from the normal standard lease form and anything that is worth flagging as maybe a little bit of a cause for concern. So like I said, rents going down or offering a bunch of free rent to get them into the building that makes the rent look artificially high. Stuff like that.
